label: Heavy going
summary: The world slipped to 38 as the fragile US-Iran pause collapsed into open war over the weekend. US Central Command launched a second and third night of strikes, hitting dozens of Iranian air-defence systems, coastal radar sites, missile and drone capabilities and small boats after a cargo ship was attacked in the Strait of Hormuz; Iranian state media reported at least one killed and four wounded at a Mahshahr water station. Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they struck US bases in Jordan, Bahrain and Kuwait, Jordan shot down four missiles crossing its airspace, and Tehran's new Strait Authority declared transit 'not possible' while Washington insisted the waterway stayed open. Oil jumped almost four percent, with Brent near 79 dollars, as markets braced for supply shocks. Violence and disaster piled up elsewhere: a pub fire in northern Bangkok killed at least 27 people and injured 63, many trapped in restrooms; DR Congo's Bundibugyo-Ebola outbreak passed 600 deaths against roughly 1,759 cases as suspected infections spread to new provinces; Sudan's RSF kept half a million people besieged in el-Obeid; Russian overnight strikes on Ukraine killed several; and flooding tied to Tropical Storm Maysak and Typhoon Bavi left dozens dead across China and the Philippines even as relief scaled up. Against the gloom, brighter threads held: the World Cup semifinals drew the top four FIFA-ranked nations for the first time ever, a California team bioprinted human kidney and liver tissue in orbit for the first time, and Europe logged cleaner air, record EV sales and a prize-winning malaria vaccine. The headline lands three points below yesterday's 41, reflecting an active-war Middle East that a 'Bad'-band read now demands.

drivers.headline: The US-Iran ceasefire collapses into open war as US Central Command launches strikes for a second straight night, hitting dozens of Iranian military targets including air-defence systems, coastal radar sites, missile and drone capabilities and small boats, with President Trump saying the US had struck Iran 'very hard' overnight; the strikes follow an attack on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz, and Iranian state media report at least one person killed and four wounded at an agricultural water station in Mahshahr

Iran's Revolutionary Guards claim they struck US military bases across the Gulf in retaliation, saying they hit Prince Hassan Air Base in Jordan, a US drone command centre in Bahrain and airbases including Ali Al Salem in Kuwait, while Jordan's military says it intercepted and shot down four missiles that entered its airspace from Iran, widening a conflict that had been paused only days earlier

The Strait of Hormuz becomes the flashpoint of the war as Iran's newly created Persian Gulf Strait Authority declares that transit is 'not possible at present,' citing what it calls illegal US military movements, while Washington and CENTCOM insist the vital oil chokepoint remains open; global oil prices jump on the standoff, with Brent crude rising close to four percent toward 79 dollars a barrel on fears of a supply shock

A fast-moving fire tears through the Na Ladprao pub in northern Bangkok early Monday, killing at least 27 people and injuring 63, with 22 in critical condition; many victims are found in the restrooms at the back of the venue, and a musician tells authorities he saw smoke from a circuit breaker near the stage and heard an explosion before the power failed, though the cause remains under investigation

The Democratic Republic of the Congo's Bundibugyo-virus Ebola outbreak, the fastest-growing the continent has recorded, passes 600 confirmed deaths against about 1,759 cases, with suspected infections now reported in the previously untouched provinces of Tshopo and Haut-Uele; there is still no approved vaccine or treatment, though a clinical trial of the monoclonal antibody MBP134 and antiviral remdesivir is under way

Sudan's Rapid Support Forces keep roughly half a million people besieged in el-Obeid, the North Kordofan capital, having seized control of nearly all surrounding routes as UN human rights chief Volker Turk documents 15 drone strikes killing at least 45 civilians in three weeks and warns the city risks becoming the next el-Fasher-style massacre; el-Obeid has been without electricity for nearly a month amid fuel shortages and soaring food prices

Russia and Ukraine trade deadly overnight air attacks as Moscow fires dozens of drones and missiles, including at Kyiv, killing at least seven people across the Kharkiv, Mykolaiv, Kherson and Kyiv regions, even as observers note Russia's summer advance has stalled and Ukraine estimates Moscow suffered nearly 40,000 casualties in June alone, far outstripping its recruitment capacity

Flooding from Tropical Storm Maysak kills 39 people in southern China, most after a dam breach inundated the city of Nanning, where 26 died and about 130,000 people were evacuated using drones and thousands of boats, while Typhoon Bavi makes landfall in Zhejiang and enhanced monsoon rains trigger landslides that kill 15 in the southern Philippines, underscoring a punishing storm season across East and Southeast Asia

Nine months into a US-brokered ceasefire, Gaza's humanitarian nightmare deepens as UN briefers tell the Security Council that Israel now controls nearly 70 percent of the enclave, no hospital is fully operational, and laboratories and blood banks verge on collapse; nearly 1,000 Palestinians have been killed since the truce began, and the sensitive second phase on disarmament and withdrawal remains unimplemented

The 2026 World Cup semifinals draw the top four FIFA-ranked nations for the first time in the tournament's history, with France, Spain, England and Argentina all reaching the last four; Argentina booked its place on a 112th-minute Julian Alvarez strike against Switzerland, setting up France against Spain in Dallas and England against Argentina in Atlanta in a globally watched climax

In a first for space medicine, California-based Auxilium Biotechnologies bioprints human kidney and liver tissue aboard the International Space Station using its AMP-1 orbital bioprinter and cell designs from the Wake Forest Institute for Regenerative Medicine, a milestone the company calls proof of the 'versatility and scalability' of in-space biomanufacturing and a step toward printing transplantable tissue

A run of quieter progress holds the floor against the week's heavier news, as Europe records air pollution falling 3-5 percent a year since 2010, battery-electric vehicles outsell petrol cars in the UK for the first time ever with 516,490 sold against 504,010, and the scientist behind the R21/Matrix-M malaria vaccine, credited with saving thousands of lives at 75-80 percent efficacy, wins the 2026 European Inventor Award

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Iran war / 2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis / 2026 Ebola epidemic / 2026 Pacific typhoon season / Gaza peace plan / Russo-Ukrainian war timeline)
